biasFlags: Speculative political theory based on anecdotal voter sentiment

entries.source: Casually Explained: Billionaires
entries.summary: Provides a humorous yet critical look at the social isolation and resource control of the world's 800+ billionaires.
keyFacts: Confiscating all U.S. wealth above $999 million would yield $6.22 trillion.
This amount would cover 11 months of government spending or an $18,000 one-time payment per person.
Notes that Peter Thiel was unable to define 'human survival' during a recent public appearance.
biasFlags: Humorous/satirical tone used for systemic critique

entries.source: why facts dont change minds (goose explains)
entries.summary: Explores the 'backfire loop,' where providing factual evidence against an identity-based belief causes individuals to entrench deeper.
keyFacts: Motivated reasoning causes the brain to protect safety over truth.
Beliefs are framed as 'armor' against social exclusion or shame.
Algorithms amplify outrage content to maintain engagement by keeping users in fear.

entries.source: How can we help people have the crack in the brainwashing?
entries.summary: Former cult member discusses how to assist individuals in deconstructing from extreme ideologies.
keyFacts: Acceptance and lack of judgment are required to prevent individuals from retreating into cults.
Asks questions rather than arguing to create 'cracks' in indoctrination.
Anticipates a 'mass deconstruction' from MAGA occurring in early 2026.
biasFlags: Explicitly categorizes political movements as 'brainwashing'

entries.source: Not today Satan...
entries.summary: Debunks the claim that the U.S. was founded as a Christian nation using constitutional and historical evidence.
keyFacts: Article 6, Clause 3 explicitly prohibits religious tests for public office.
The 1797 Treaty of Tripoli states the U.S. is not founded on the Christian religion.
James Madison designed the separation of church and state to avoid European religious strife.

entries.source: Why Centrism Doesnt Work
entries.summary: Uses a sports team analogy to explain why the left cannot win by compromising with conservative voters.
keyFacts: Argues conservative political identity is rooted in 'home team' loyalty rather than performance.
Claims compromising on human rights makes the compromising party 'more like' the opposition and weakens them.
Advocates for doubling down on pro-worker and pro-rural development policies to win over non-voters.
biasFlags: Highly partisan dismissal of compromise as a political tactic

entries.source: How many of the 14 can you identify?
entries.summary: Outlines 14 distinct characteristics of a fascist regime, including nationalism, suppression of rights, and state-corporate fusion.
keyFacts: Identifies 'identification of enemies as a unifying cause' as a core fascist trait.
Notes the suppression of labor unions and the protection of corporate power as systemic markers.
Cites fraudulent elections and the demonization of intellectuals as late-stage indicators.

entries.source: ICE doesnt want you to see this ad. | Kat Abughazaleh for Illinois
entries.summary: Campaign ad highlighting ICE enforcement actions in Chicago and calling for community resistance against warrantless removals.
keyFacts: Claims ICE is abducting individuals and detaining grandmothers on concrete floors.
Labels the current ICE protocols as a violation of the Geneva Conventions.
Campaign states it receives no funding from corporate PACs or groups like AIPAC.
biasFlags: Hyperbolic campaign rhetoric designed for emotional impact

entries.source: THE RIGHT IS CHANGING
entries.summary: Discusses the process of helping family members disengage from far-right indoctrination through patience and 'love-led' conversations.
keyFacts: Argues 'moral purity culture' on both sides prevents national healing.
Suggests right-leaning individuals are starting to discuss the reality of living in a 'fascist state.'
Places responsibility on white allies to engage with white families who have been radicalized.
biasFlags: Identitarian framing of responsibility based on race

entries.source: Scott Adams: Too Dumb to Live
entries.summary: Retrospective on Dilbert creator Scott Adams, following his death, focusing on his transition to far-right conspiracy theories.
keyFacts: Adams reportedly refused standard medical treatment for prostate cancer in favor of ivermectin.
Dilbert creator claimed to have 'hypnotized' himself using ChatGPT.
Faced widespread backlash for labeling Black people a 'hate group' in 2023.
biasFlags: Hostile tone; uses individual death as a platform for ideological critique

entries.source: Why MAGA billionaires want you miserable
entries.summary: Uses psychological data to argue that the billionaire class demonizes empathy because supporting public infrastructure is expensive for them.
keyFacts: A 2019 study showed progressives have a wider 'scope of empathy' (ranking at 14/16) than conservatives (ranking at 3/16).
Peter Thiel reportedly stated religion is used to scare the GOP base into defending elite wealth.
The meeting of basic human needs diminishes the fear that authoritarians rely on for control.
biasFlags: Reduces political opposition to a psychological deficiency in empathy

entries.source: Breaking the Wheel: How American Mythology Kills American Resistance
entries.summary: Argues that Americans learn 'mythology' rather than 'history,' leading to a perpetual state of political infancy.
keyFacts: Jill Lepore's work highlights how historians abandoning national studies allowed 'liberal nationalist mythology' to grow.
Mythology erases the disruptive reality of figures like MLK and Frederick Douglass, turning them into safe inspirations.
Traditional narratives attribute failure to 'bad actors' rather than 'bad design' of the system.
biasFlags: Critical/Revisionist historical framing

entries.source: Kat On CNN: Progressives Candidates Are Providing Solutions | Kat Abughazaleh For Illinois
entries.summary: Abughazaleh discusses JD Vance's role in the Epstein files fallout and argues that progressive candidates offer practical solutions.
keyFacts: Notes a 2021 tweet from JD Vance claiming journalists should be ashamed for not asking about Epstein.
Merrick Garland is critiqued for not allowing potentially damaging names in the Epstein files to leak.
Progressive platforms emphasize building more housing and implementing universal healthcare as 'workable' goals.
biasFlags: Self-promotional campaign appearance

entries.source: 8 Weird Thinking Patterns That Actually Reveal Genius
entries.summary: Identifies cognitive traits like 'mentally zooming out' and 'flipping perspectives' as indicators of high intelligence.
keyFacts: Self-generated dialectical thinking involves arguing with your own opinions to test logic.
Metacognition is the sense of watching yourself participate in a situation in real-time.
Divergent thinking is linked to the impulse to break 'invisible constraints' or nonsensical rules.

entries.source: How to convince your friends to back wealth taxes
entries.summary: Gary Stevenson provides a strategy for persuading others on wealth taxes, focusing on 'protecting your grandchildren.'
keyFacts: Differentiates between taxing high earners (who move) and taxing assets (which are tied to the country).
The 'Squeeze Out' explains how the top 1% aggressively expands their share, reducing the total available for others.
Advocates for a 'human amplification model' to bypass billionaire-funded media channels.
biasFlags: Uses a specific 'Squeeze Out' economic model as objective fact

entries.source: CEOs keep DYING and Mainstream Media won't talk about it
entries.summary: Discusses the deaths of several high-profile CEOs, framing the lack of media coverage as a sign of the widening class divide.
keyFacts: Nicholas Manning (HCA) died in Baltimore; HCA was involved in the largest healthcare fraud case in U.S. history.
Frederick Smith (FedEx founder) died at 80; noted for a 1975 forgery indictment and a fatal hit-and-run.
Blackstone executive killed in a shooting; Blackstone is the largest commercial landlord in history.
biasFlags: Highly populist and resentful tone; implies cosmic retribution

entries.source: A Bold Idea to Rebuild the Working Class | Molly Hemstreet | TED
entries.summary: Molly Hemstreet details the creation of 'The Industrial Commons' in southern Appalachia to revitalize the textile industry through employee ownership.
keyFacts: 'Material Return' project turns textile waste back into yarn, increasing value from cents to $9 per pound.
'Opportunity Threads' is a worker-owned upcycling facility processing thousands of shirts weekly.
The project aims to create 'rooted wealth' that cannot be extracted by outside corporations.

entries.source: Five Ways to Fight Authoritarianism NOW
entries.summary: Outlines five strategies for individuals to actively resist authoritarian centralized control by building local and economic power.
keyFacts: Encourages building 'economic resistance' by boycotting corporations that fund authoritarian candidates.
Advocates for creating 'mutual aid pods' to reduce dependency on failing state systems.
Identifies 'staying human and grounded' as a critical resistance tool against fear and burnout.

entries.source: How + Why Did Big Tech Turn To The Right? - SOME MORE NEWS
entries.summary: Argues that tech giants have become a 'fourth branch of government,' wielding power through digital infrastructure and surveillance.
keyFacts: Tech billionaires have shifted right as a defensive reaction to potential antitrust regulation and 'woke' criticism.
Companies stifle innovation by acquiring disruptive startups to prevent competition.
Critiques Amazon's media production as propaganda portraying corporations as societal saviors.
biasFlags: Strong partisan framing of tech leaders' ideological shifts

entries.source: Is this how we stop infighting on The Left?
entries.summary: Discusses the necessity of a 'big tent' approach to combating fascism, while acknowledging barriers to entry in political groups.
keyFacts: Permaculture ethics (earth care, people care, fair share) are framed as fundamentally anti-capitalist.
Advocates for 'ecumenical' partnerships with Never-Trump Republicans and centrists for the singular goal of stopping fascism.
Suggests that infighting on the left stems from a lack of shared definitions of 'leftism' vs 'neoliberalism.'

entries.source: What should we do about evil people?
entries.summary: Analyzes belief formation as a product of environmental inputs vs internal processing, questioning the 'carceral approach' to punishing bad beliefs.
keyFacts: If beliefs are chosen, the logical response is punishment; if beliefs are environmental, the response is changing the environment.
Notes that billions are spent on creating 'false inputs' to sustain misinformation.
Grassroots sharing of 'true inputs' on social media is identified as a successful counter-strategy.

entries.source: Andor: The Death of Truth
entries.summary: Uses the Star Wars show 'Andor' to illustrate how oppressive regimes create criminals through over-legislation and surveillance.
keyFacts: The Empire's 'public order re-sentencing directive' mirrors modern authoritarian crackdowns on dissent.
Argues that the 'death of truth' is a prerequisite for systemic evil to flourish.
Framing rebellion as a natural response to the erasure of local customs and traditions.
biasFlags: Heavy reliance on fictional allegory to make real-world political points

entries.source: This guy really lays it out!
entries.summary: Explores the cognitive dissonance in white evangelical Christianity, arguing it uses biblical narratives to justify violence.
keyFacts: A 'narcissistic cycle' is created where actions have no accountability as long as the perpetrator asks for divine forgiveness.
Adherents view external criticism as 'hate' against their way of life rather than a response to material harm.
Argues that the belief in a single 'right way' to heaven necessitates dehumanizing those who do not align.
biasFlags: Strong anti-religious framing; pathologizes evangelical theology

entries.source: CRITICAL MESSAGE ABOUT CULT WAKING UP
entries.summary: Describes the intense panic within fundamentalist groups as they confront external information.
keyFacts: Fundamentalist education focuses on viewing 'the left' as a satanic threat, inducing biological fear responses.
Mormons are reported to be in a state of higher panic than at any other point in the speaker's memory.
Kindness and 'grace' are identified as the only tools capable of helping individuals transition safely out of the group.
biasFlags: Patronizing framing of religious groups as emotionally infantile

entries.source: Texas Democrat Drops BREATHTAKING Speech
entries.summary: James Talarico argues for political unity by focusing on shared material struggles rather than partisan labels.
keyFacts: Focusing on everyday concerns like living wages can lead to agreement between MAGA and progressive voters.
Billionaire-owned algorithms are identified as the primary drivers of artificial social division.
Notes that James Talarico's appearance on Joe Rogan's podcast successfully reached a broad conservative audience.
biasFlags: Optimistic framing of 'common ground' may underestimate fundamental ideological rifts

entries.source: CULT MEMBERS AS THEY WAKE UP
entries.summary: Explains why individuals leaving cults keep their 'awakening' private to avoid ostracization.
keyFacts: Leaving a religious cult does not immediately change political identity; the speaker remained Republican for years after leaving Mormonism.
Public shaming on social media reinforces the cult's narrative that the outside world is hateful.
Unity should be prioritized around shared opposition to 'the elite' rather than past statements.

entries.source: What Democrats Refuse to Learn from Zohran Mamdani (w/ Hasan Piker)
entries.summary: Hasan Piker and Current Affairs discuss the suppression of anti-Zionist voices and media's failure to address material harm of U.S. foreign policy.
keyFacts: Hasan Piker uses media profiles as 'terrorism insurance' against smear campaigns from the ADL.
Richie Torres is cited as a Democratic congressman who attempted to have Piker deplatformed from Twitch.
Reading the 'words of official enemies' is argued as necessary for understanding the rationale behind political violence.
biasFlags: Strongly anti-establishment and anti-Zionist framing

entries.source: Progressive Politics: Comedian Bill Burr Drops FACTS On How White People Fall For Billionaires!!
entries.summary: Analyzes how economic hardship is falsely attributed to immigrants by wealthy elites to distract from their own refusal to pay living wages.
keyFacts: Argues that the current 'illegal bounty hunter' behavior of ICE sets a dangerous precedent for domestic law enforcement.
Claims that the super-rich hoard resources even though there is enough food and shelter to support the entire population.
Asserts that removing undocumented workers would not lead to higher wages for citizens, as employers would likely relocate factories overseas.
biasFlags: Strong populist framing
Uses a comedian's commentary as primary evidence for systemic economic trends

entries.source: They STILL Don't Get It: The Left's Branding Problem
entries.summary: A right-leaning critique of progressive culture, arguing that the left has abandoned health, fitness, and traditional family values.
keyFacts: Critiques the New York Times for profiling a leftist streamer as a 'progressive mind in a MAGA body.'
Argues that the normalization of obesity and the rejection of traditional beauty standards alienates moderate voters.
Claims the left's 'oppressor versus oppressed' framework prevents individual self-accountability.
biasFlags: Strong right-wing ideological bias
Uses emotive labels like 'degeneracy' and 'sterility' to describe political opposition

entries.source: The American Dream is falling apart | Derek Thompson: Full Interview
entries.summary: Derek Thompson examines the 'Anti-Social Century,' detailing how technological advances have decimated face-to-face social interaction.
keyFacts: The NIH is critiqued for 'conservative funding practices' that favor established scientists over high-reward breakthroughs.
Proposes 'lotteries for grants' to reduce the time scientists spend on administrative paperwork.
Links high housing costs in progressive states directly to high homelessness rates.

entries.source: Is this what we've been missing as we oppose oligarchy?
entries.summary: Uses Timothy Snyder's work on freedom to argue that unpredictability and 'why' questions are essential for resisting surveillance capitalism.
keyFacts: Argues that surveillance capitalism transforms humans into 'how' creatures (efficiency) rather than 'why' creatures (values).
Claims that believing everything is 'predictable' is a form of collaboration with one's own unfreedom.
Identifies human judgment and virtues as the 'fifth dimension' that resists digital machine logic.

entries.source: The Blue Road to Trump Hell: Norm Solomon on How Corporate Democrats Paved the Way for Autocracy
entries.summary: Norman Solomon analyzes the Democratic Party's failure to self-examine, attributing it to corporate capture and a repetitive compulsion to serve elite power.
keyFacts: The Harris campaign reportedly spent $1.5 billion in 107 days primarily on consultants and TV ads.
Retrospective polling suggests an arms embargo stance on Israel could have flipped critical swing states in 2024.
Corporate Democrats are critiqued for failing to deliver material gains, thereby creating a vacuum for fascistic politics.
biasFlags: Explicitly anti-establishment/progressive framing

entries.source: A Strategy to End the Trump Regime
entries.summary: Proposes a strategic counter-maneuver to authoritarianism by targeting the 'supply lines' (finance, consulting, media) that sustain it.
keyFacts: Characterizes the regime as using 'Veruca tactics' (impulsive greed) that lead to strategic overextension.
Argues that resistance should focus on the bond market and domestic supply lines like law firms and tech infrastructure.
Notes that the regime's power fundamentally relies on the ongoing consent of regular workers in these sectors.

entries.source: Noam Chomsky - How the Elite controls you
entries.summary: Chomsky traces the history of 'Thought Control' in democratic societies, where elites manage public opinion when overt force is no longer viable.
keyFacts: Founded on John Jay's principle: 'Those who own the country ought to govern it.'
Identifies 'Manufacturing Consent' as the core function of the public relations and advertising industries.
Describes the Trilateral Commission's 1960s 'Crisis of Democracy' as a reaction to too much public engagement.

entries.source: Noam Chomsky: The five filters of the mass media machine | The Listening Post
entries.summary: Outlines the 'Manufacturing Consent' framework, explaining how five systemic filters shape news to serve elite interests.
keyFacts: The five filters: Ownership (corporate profit), Advertising (funding source), Establishment (reliance on official sources), Flak (disciplining dissent), and Common Enemy (unifying via fear).
Argues that media functions as a propaganda tool to marginalize the public from political decision-making.
Corporate ownership ensures that news content does not threaten the fundamental structures of capitalism.

entries.source: Let's talk about meaningless hope, the path, and you....
entries.summary: Critiques abstract optimism, proposing instead the creation of local community networks as the only viable path to survive a de facto dictatorship.
keyFacts: Local networks are intended to mitigate immediate material harm (climate change, policy shifts) while forming new voting blocs.
Argues that relying on Washington D.C. politicians to fix systemic issues is ineffective because they are part of the system.
Systemic change is framed as requiring a shift in people's minds at the hyper-local level rather than just top-down legislative wins.

entries.source: Why cant the right see Trump for who he is?
entries.summary: Explains the biological basis for cult attachment, arguing that emotional devotion to a leader suppresses the brain's reasoning centers.
keyFacts: Attachment to a political leader triggers oxytocin and dopamine releases similar to 'falling in love.'
When these neurochemicals are active, the prefrontal cortex (responsible for logic) becomes less active.
Educational goal is to teach the public how to 'fish' for psychological understanding of their own conditioning.
biasFlags: Reductionist biological framing of political support

entries.source: stephen sells out
entries.summary: Criticizes the "get the bag at any cost" mentality using Steven A. Smith's promotion of Solitaire Cash app. Parent company Papaya Gaming faces lawsuit for using bots to rig games. Smith turned embarrassing solitaire-during-NBA-finals moment into paid gambling promotion.
keyFacts: Steven A. Smith promoting Solitaire Cash app linked to rigged game allegations
Papaya Gaming used "liquidity bots" and "tailored bots" to predetermine game outcomes per court documents
Company repeatedly denied using bots despite court evidence
One player earned only $100 after playing for over a year
Smith expressed indifference about Epstein client list, cited as evidence of self-centered approach
"Beat Steven" campaign paid influencers with #ad hashtags
biasFlags: Anti-celebrity-endorsement framing but well-evidenced with court documents

entries.source: MAGA WAKING UP
entries.summary: Discusses fracturing of Trump support due to visa policy comments and perceived lack of "America first" focus. Applies cult psychology framework — when cult leaders fail to provide safety, trust erodes. Advocates empathetic engagement with departing MAGA supporters rather than confrontation.
keyFacts: MAGA discontent driven by Trump visa comments suggesting lack of American talent, not just Epstein case
Cult psychology: leaders must maintain sense of safety — when it erodes, followers leave
Growing House movement with defectors signing discharge letter potentially creating veto-proof majority
Multiple fracture points: Israel issues, Epstein situation, visa policy alienating different groups simultaneously
Recommends treating departing MAGA members as people who received "bad directions" not bad people
Compares cult membership to abusive relationship where judgment is clouded by emotion
biasFlags: Sympathetic to progressive perspective but offers genuinely useful cult psychology framework applicable across ideologies

entries.source: THE FIVE PANICS OVER SOCIALISM SPREADING IN THE US
entries.summary: Analyzes rise of socialist sentiment in Democratic party through DSA electoral wins and AOC's populist appeal. Discusses Republican anxiety about a potential "FDR 2.0" figure emerging from the left. Notes DSA systematically building ranks similar to Turning Point USA on the right.
keyFacts: Recent DSA wins: Zoran Momah in NYC, Katie Wilson potentially in Seattle
Socialist wing forces Republicans to distinguish between "normal" and "socialist" Democrats, defending centrists
Fetterman argues establishment Democrats should lean into left flank as strategic threat
Republicans fear Trump-style transformational figure emerging from socialist left (FDR 2.0 scenario)
DSA described as systematically building institutional presence similar to Turning Point USA
Peter Thiel acknowledged understanding why younger people drawn to socialism due to perceived capitalism failures
Speaker argues identity politics has always existed on the right, typically focused on white men
biasFlags: Mixed ideological framing — includes both progressive and conservative commentary on socialism. HasanAbi channel has strong left perspective but this video includes Fox News clips providing right-wing counterpoints.

entries.source: White Working-Class Realignment 1968-2024 - Research Synthesis
entries.summary: Comprehensive review of why white working-class voters shifted Republican. Racial resentment and cultural displacement are far stronger predictors than economic hardship. Thomas Frank's 'duped by social issues' thesis is overstated per Bartels' counter-analysis.
keyFacts: Sides/Tesler/Vavreck (Identity Crisis, 2018): White non-college voters who felt 'like a stranger in their own land' were 3.5x more likely to support Trump, net of economic hardship
Economic hardship (job loss, low income) was associated with LESS Trump support; economic fatalism (system is rigged) predicted MORE Trump support
PRRI/Atlantic 2016-2017: Racial resentment, anti-immigrant attitudes, and cultural displacement are far stronger predictors than personal economic stress in regression models
Larry Bartels (2006, Unequal Democracy 2008): The big shift was white Southerners high in racial conservatism, NOT a broad white working-class Republican shift outside the South
Thomas Frank (What's the Matter with Kansas, 2004): Argued working class 'duped' by social issues (abortion, guns, religion) - later criticized as overstated
Bartels found income growth under Democrats was higher for lower/middle-income voters; Republicans delivered more to the top
biasFlags: Well-established academic consensus that racial/cultural factors dominate over purely economic explanations. Some political scientists argue deindustrialization created structural conditions for racialized politics.

entries.source: Fox News Effect on Voting - DellaVigna/Kaplan Study
entries.summary: Quantified causal effect of Fox News on Republican vote share using staggered cable rollout 1996-2000. Fox News persuaded 3-28% of its viewers to vote Republican who otherwise would not have - enough to swing close races.
keyFacts: DellaVigna & Kaplan (QJE, 2007): Exploited staggered Fox News rollout across cable systems 1996-2000
Fox News exposure increased Republican presidential vote share by 0.4-0.7 percentage points at town level in 2000 election
Estimated 3-28% of Fox viewers were persuaded to vote Republican who otherwise would not have
Also found increased Republican Senate vote share and turnout in treated areas - persuasion effect, not just mobilization
Later studies find similar order-of-magnitude effects in low single-digit percentage range - large enough to swing close races
Used controls and fixed effects to address whether already-Republican places simply adopted Fox faster
biasFlags: Well-established causal study published in top economics journal. Ecological inference limitations acknowledged but methodology is rigorous.

entries.source: Deep Canvassing - What Actually Changes Minds
entries.summary: Standard partisan canvassing has essentially zero persuasion effect in modern elections. Deep canvassing (10-15 min empathetic conversations) produces small but real, durable attitude changes - the only field-tested method that works.
keyFacts: Broockman & Kalla (Science, 2016): 10-15 minute non-judgmental conversations about transgender rights reduced transphobia with effects detectable months later
Effect sizes comparable to or larger than many partisan campaign ads, with unusual durability
Kalla & Broockman (2017 meta-analysis): Standard canvassing, phone calls, and mail in general elections almost never change vote choice - average effects essentially zero
Deep canvassing works via active listening, perspective-taking, and personal storytelling - NOT scripts or fact-dumping
Success rates modest (move a few percentage points of contacted group) but far higher than ~0 impact of traditional canvassing
Most strong effects documented on specific prejudices or low-information ballot issues, not hardened party ID
biasFlags: Well-established experimental evidence. Whether it scales to hardened partisan attitudes remains contested.

entries.source: Progressive Policies That Poll 60%+ With All Voters
entries.summary: Multiple progressive economic policies command supermajority support when framed concretely without partisan labels. Support drops when framed as Biden's plan or Democratic agenda - framing matters more than policy substance.
keyFacts: Medicare drug negotiation: ~72% support (Politico/Morning Consult 2021), ~70-71% among moderates and non-college voters
Paid family and medical leave: ~70-72% support, strong backing among moderates and non-college voters (~70%)
Background checks on all gun sales: 80-90% overall, Republican support usually above 70% - stable across 2010s-2020s
Raising federal minimum wage: 60-70% support overall depending on level ($15 vs smaller steps), Republican support high 30s to low 50s
Taxing billionaires/wealth surtaxes: 60-70% support nationally, GOP support often near or above 40%
Infrastructure spending (roads, bridges, broadband): 70%+ routinely including solid Republican majorities
CRITICAL: Support drops significantly when partisan cues are added (e.g., 'Biden's plan') - framing determines support
biasFlags: Polling numbers are real but context-dependent - support varies by question wording, party labels, and specific dollar amounts.

entries.source: Corporate Psychopathy - Hare/Babiak Research
entries.summary: Psychopathic traits are ~4x more common in corporate leadership than general population. Corporate psychopaths rise through charisma and impression management while harming organizations - they interview well but perform poorly as team players.
keyFacts: General population psychopathy estimated at ~1% using Hare's Psychopathy Checklist-Revised (PCL-R)
Babiak, Neumann & Hare (2010): In 203 corporate professionals at a U.S. company, 3.9-4% met psychopathy cutoff - roughly 4x the general population rate
Psychopathy scores correlated POSITIVELY with charisma, creativity, and communication ratings
Same scores correlated NEGATIVELY with team-player ratings, responsibility, and overall performance
Corporate psychopaths rise via impression management while actively harming organizational function
Robert Hare is the leading researcher - developed the PCL-R, the gold standard for psychopathy assessment
biasFlags: Single firm, small sample (203 professionals) - external validity concerns. Pop commentary often overstates the prevalence.

entries.source: Tobacco-to-Fossil-Fuel Deception Pipeline
entries.summary: Tobacco companies knew of cancer risks by 1950s, denied them for decades, paid $206B+ in settlements. Fossil fuel companies used the EXACT same playbook - Exxon scientist James Black told management in July 1977 that CO2 would warm the planet, then company funded doubt campaigns from late 1980s onward.
keyFacts: Tobacco: 1950 BMJ paper linked smoking to lung cancer; 1954 British Doctors Study and 1964 Surgeon General Report established connection
Tobacco internal documents from 1950s onward show company scientists warning management of carcinogenic risks and nicotine addiction
Master Settlement Agreement (1998): tobacco companies agreed to pay at least $206 billion over first 25 years; proposed 1997 settlement would have been $365.5 billion
Exxon: Senior scientist James Black told management committee in July 1977 there was 'general scientific agreement' that CO2 emissions would warm planet
Exxon funded sophisticated in-house climate models and research in late 1970s-1980s confirming warming projections
From late 1980s-1990s: Exxon and other majors funded industry groups and think tanks to emphasize uncertainty, attack climate models, lobby against regulation
Same 'manufacturing doubt' strategy documented in both industries - directly parallel playbooks
biasFlags: Extremely well-documented through internal corporate documents, court proceedings, and investigative journalism.

entries.source: Revolving Door - Congress/Regulators to Industry
entries.summary: Large share of departing Congress members take lobbying or industry advocacy jobs in sectors they oversaw. Tech platforms dramatically increased lobbying and hired former congressional staff as regulation loomed.
keyFacts: Large share of departing Congress members take lobbying or industry advocacy jobs, especially in sectors they oversaw (SEC, FCC, FDA)
Brookings: As Congress considers American Innovation and Choice Online Act, Amazon/Apple/Meta/Google dramatically increased lobbying expenditures
Same platforms hired many former congressional and agency staff - revolving door accelerates during regulatory threat
Pattern documented across financial regulation, telecom, pharma, and tech sectors
Causal links from specific career moves to policy outcomes are hard to prove cleanly but pattern is systematic
biasFlags: Pattern is well-documented but direct causation is hard to prove. Both progressive and libertarian critics flag this issue.

entries.source: Regulation Works - Finance, Workplace Safety, Environment
entries.summary: Strong regulation in finance, workplace safety, and environment is associated with fewer crises, injuries, and deaths. Glass-Steagall era (1933-1999) had zero nationwide bank panics. OSHA dramatically reduced workplace deaths. Clean Air Act prevented hundreds of thousands of premature deaths while GDP continued to grow.
keyFacts: Glass-Steagall (1933-1999): Zero nationwide bank panics during strong regulation era; 1999 repeal followed by 2007-2008 financial crisis
OSHA (created 1970): Fatal and non-fatal workplace injury rates per worker fell dramatically over subsequent decades
Clean Air Act (1970, amended 1990): Key pollutants (SO2, NOx, particulates, lead) dropped sharply after implementation
EPA estimates hundreds of thousands of avoided premature deaths and millions of avoided illnesses from Clean Air Act
GDP continued to grow during all three regulatory regimes - regulation did not prevent economic expansion
Cost-benefit analyses for major environmental and safety rules usually show large net social gains
biasFlags: Directionally well-established. Opponents highlight compliance costs; data supports net positive outcomes.

entries.source: Progressive Policies Conservatives Now Defend
entries.summary: Every major progressive policy was fiercely opposed by conservatives at adoption - Social Security called 'socialism,' Medicare called 'socialized medicine' (Reagan recorded messages opposing it). All are now broadly accepted or actively defended by the same political tradition.
keyFacts: Social Security (1935): Opposed by Republicans and business groups as 'socialism'; now both parties pledge to 'protect' it
Medicare (1965): AMA and conservatives warned of 'socialized medicine'; Reagan recorded messages opposing it. Now Republicans accuse Democrats of 'cutting Medicare'
Interstate Highway System (1956): Some conservatives objected to federal spending; later Republican administrations touted highways as achievements
Civil Rights Act (1964): Strongly opposed by Southern Democrats and many conservatives; now explicit opposition is rare
Clean Air Act (1970): Resisted by industry and conservative lawmakers; few mainstream politicians now call for repeal
40-hour work week (FLSA 1938): Business groups predicted economic ruin; now broadly accepted
Child labor laws (early 20th century, FLSA): Opposed by manufacturers as interference with family autonomy
Women's suffrage (19th Amendment, 1920): Fought by conservatives and business interests; now universally celebrated
biasFlags: Historical facts are well-documented. Contemporaneous debates show deep opposition to core ideas, not just 'overreach.'

entries.source: Economic Performance - Democratic vs Republican Presidents Since 1945
entries.summary: Post-WWII data consistently shows faster GDP growth, more job creation, better stock returns, and more equal income distribution under Democratic presidents. Pattern is persistent across 80 years though causality is debated.
keyFacts: Real GDP growth: Higher average annual growth under Democratic administrations than Republican from 1945 through 2010s (BEA data)
Job creation: BLS data show more net jobs added per year under Democrats
Deficits: Despite 'small government' branding, Republican administrations ran large deficits (early 1980s, early 2000s, late 2010s)
Democratic periods often saw deficit reduction or surpluses (e.g., late 1990s Clinton surpluses)
Stock market returns historically stronger on average during Democratic presidencies
Under Democrats, income gains more evenly distributed across quintiles; under Republicans, gains concentrated at the top
Causality contested - presidents inherit business cycles, Fed policy, global shocks - but pattern is remarkably consistent
biasFlags: Pattern is real and persistent. Causality is genuinely contested - use as ammunition but acknowledge complexity.

entries.source: Nordic Model vs US - Outcomes Comparison
entries.summary: Nordic countries consistently outperform the US on life expectancy, infant mortality, social mobility, happiness, incarceration, education, and median wealth - while spending less on healthcare. The US is an outlier among rich nations for worse outcomes at higher cost.
keyFacts: Life expectancy and infant mortality: Nordics higher life expectancy, lower infant mortality despite spending smaller share of GDP on health
US is an outlier among rich nations for worse health outcomes at higher cost
Social mobility: Intergenerational mobility significantly higher in Nordic countries
World Happiness Report regularly ranks Nordic nations at or near the top; US ranks lower
US incarceration rates several times higher than Nordic countries, even accounting for crime differences
Nordic education systems perform at or above US levels with narrower rich-poor gaps
Typical Nordic households have higher median net worth than US households (US mean is higher due to extreme top-end concentration)
Skeptics cite cultural homogeneity and smaller scale; supporters emphasize institutions and policy design
biasFlags: Outcome data is well-established. Transferability debate is legitimate but often used as rhetorical shield.

entries.source: Wages, Unions, CEO Pay, Inequality 1945-2024
entries.summary: From 1945 to late 1970s, wages rose with productivity, unions peaked at ~33%, CEO pay was low double digits ratio. After deliberate policy shifts in late 1970s-1980s, wages stagnated, CEO pay exploded to hundreds-to-one, and inequality soared. The productivity-wage link broke exactly when labor power was weakened.
keyFacts: 1945-late 1970s: Real wages rose in line with productivity; union membership peaked at ~33% of workers
CEO-to-worker pay ratios were in the low double digits during high-union era
Late 1970s-1980s: Union density fell sharply due to hostile NLRB, right-to-work laws, permissive antitrust
Real wage growth for non-college workers slowed or stagnated while productivity continued rising - the link broke
CEO-to-worker pay ratios exploded to hundreds-to-one
Top 1% and 0.1% income shares climbed steadily from early 1980s onward
Key policy inflection: late 1970s/early 1980s shift to deregulation, weakened labor protections, tax reforms favoring capital
Progressive economists emphasize institutions (unions, minimum wage, tax policy); others stress market forces
biasFlags: Structural story well-established in data. Relative weight of policy choices vs market forces is genuinely debated.

entries.source: What's the Matter with Kansas? (Thomas Frank)
entries.summary: Core thesis explaining why working-class voters in Kansas shifted from leftist populism to the GOP by prioritizing 'Great Backlash' cultural grievances over economic self-interest.
keyFacts: The shift turned Kansas from a state of 1890s radicalism to one where the GOP won all 105 counties in the 2000 presidential election.
Voters focus on 'God, Guns, and Gays' to the point of supporting policies like the 2003 tax cuts that stripped $1.1B from state services.
Frank identifies a 'latte-sipping liberal' stereotype used to frame economic elites as cultural victims.
biasFlags: Criticized by some political scientists for underestimating the role of race vs. class culture.

entries.source: Identity Crisis (Sides, Tesler, Vavreck) - Detailed Metrics
entries.summary: Detailed metrics from Identity Crisis showing racial resentment scale effects on 2016 vote choice - complements Perplexity entry with specific numbers.
keyFacts: A change from minimum to maximum on 'racial resentment scale' = 31% increase in likelihood of voting for Trump.
In 2016, 61% of white voters without a college degree voted for Trump, compared to 45% for Romney in 2012.
Racial attitudes were more strongly correlated with vote choice in 2016 than in any election from 1988-2012.
biasFlags: The 'racial resentment' scale is occasionally criticized by conservatives for conflating policy views with prejudice.

entries.source: Loss Aversion in Politics (Alesina & Passarelli)
entries.summary: Explains 'status quo bias' where voters fear the loss of current benefits more than they value potential gains from new policies.
keyFacts: Voters exhibit 'political endowment effects' - opposition to ACA flips to support once it becomes the status quo.
Loss aversion makes majorities prefer 'prudent' voting rules (supermajorities) to prevent radical changes.
52% of voters might support a policy in the abstract but vote against it when it threatens the current equilibrium.
biasFlags: Based on mathematical modeling of median voter behavior.

entries.source: Last-Place Aversion (Kuziemko, Buell, Reich, Norton)
entries.summary: Experimental evidence that low-income individuals oppose redistribution because they want to maintain their status above the 'bottom' group.
keyFacts: Individuals making just above minimum wage are the MOST likely to oppose a minimum wage increase ($7.25 to $15).
In 'dictator games,' players in second-to-last place were 50% more likely to give money to the person above them than below them.
Respondents support redistribution significantly less when told it would move a lower-ranked group into their same income tier.
biasFlags: Findings based on laboratory experiments; real-world political application is hypothesized extension.

entries.source: Religious Right Timeline: Falwell to Project 2025
entries.summary: Chronology of evangelical capture of the GOP, shifting from 'segregation academies' to judicial dominance.
keyFacts: 1979: Jerry Falwell founds Moral Majority after Paul Weyrich identifies 'segregation academies' as more potent mobilization issue than abortion.
1980: Moral Majority credited with registering 2 million new voters to support Ronald Reagan.
2024: Project 2025 includes 900+ pages detailing replacement of 50,000 civil servants with ideologically aligned personnel.
biasFlags: Historical narrative emphasizes strategic 'segregation' roots often denied by movement leaders.

entries.source: Union Decline & Republican Shift (EPI / Pew)
entries.summary: Statistical correlation between collapse of union density and loss of Democratic identity in the Rust Belt.
keyFacts: US union density dropped from 20.1% in 1983 to 9.9% in 2024.
White union household Democratic identification dropped from 46% in 1960s to 34% by 2020.
In 2020, white union members were as likely to identify as Republican (33%) as Democrat (34%) for first time in 60 years.
biasFlags: Correlation does not equal causation, though experts cite loss of 'union as political school' effect.

entries.source: Social Identity Theory (Tajfel & Turner)
entries.summary: Foundational theory explaining why voters choose candidates that validate their group identity even if they harm their individual economic standing.
keyFacts: 'Minimal Group Paradigm': people will sacrifice absolute gains for their own group to maximize the relative gap over an out-group.
Identity-based voting acts as a 'psychological wage' (W.E.B. Du Bois), providing status that compensates for material poverty.
Group membership reduces 'cognitive load' - voters use party labels as heuristic to avoid researching 50+ complex policy issues.
biasFlags: A general psychological theory applied to politics, not a specific political study.

entries.source: Successful Psychopaths in Hierarchies
entries.summary: Mechanism by which Machiavellianism and 'Fearless Dominance' allow sociopaths to climb corporate and political ladders.
keyFacts: 'Fearless Dominance' (psychopathy sub-trait) positively correlated with high presidential performance ratings (e.g., LBJ, Teddy Roosevelt).
Psychopaths rated as having 25% better 'first impressions' and charisma than non-psychopathic peers in hiring scenarios.
Institutions often confuse 'ruthlessness' with 'decisiveness,' promoting low-empathy individuals to high-stakes roles.
biasFlags: 'Successful psychopath' is a clinical designation, not a moral judgment.

entries.source: The Exxon Knowledge (Supran & Oreskes, Science)
entries.summary: Analysis of ExxonMobil's internal global warming projections from 1977-2003 vs. their public denial campaigns.
keyFacts: Exxon internal models from 1977-2003 accurately projected warming of 0.2C per decade, matching actual observed temperatures.
83% of Exxon's peer-reviewed internal papers acknowledged climate change was real, yet 81% of their public 'advertorials' expressed doubt.
Exxon spent over $16M between 1998-2005 funding 40+ organizations to promote climate skepticism.
biasFlags: ExxonMobil disputes 'cherry-picking' of documents by researchers.

entries.source: The Pharma Revolving Door (OpenSecrets Data)
entries.summary: Data tracking movement of government regulators into pharmaceutical lobbying roles.
keyFacts: In 2018, pharma sector employed 1,021 'revolving door' lobbyists who previously worked for federal government.
Pharma spent a record $282M on lobbying in 2018, more than any other industry.
Firms hiring former regulators see average 4.2-day reduction in approval times, saving up to $8.4M per drug.
biasFlags: Revolving door lobbyists argue their 'expertise' is what is being hired, not 'influence'.

entries.source: Normalization of Deviance (The Torture Memos)
entries.summary: How legal 'sociopathy' is normalized through bureaucratic jargon, exemplified by the 2002 'Bybee Memo'.
keyFacts: 2002 'Bybee Memo' redefined torture so physical pain must be 'equivalent in intensity to organ failure' to be illegal.
This linguistic shift allowed CIA to use 10 specific 'enhanced interrogation' techniques on detainees at black sites.
Sociologists call this 'structural sociopathy' - individual actors follow rules that result in collective atrocities.
biasFlags: Legal interpretations highly polarized between human rights groups and executive power advocates.

entries.source: Regulatory Capture in Patent Law (Tabakovic & Wollmann)
entries.summary: Evidence that patent examiners are more lenient toward firms that subsequently hire them.
keyFacts: 'Revolving door' patent examiners grant 12.6% to 17.6% more patents to firms that later become their employers.
Patents granted by 'revolving' examiners receive 21% to 27% fewer citations, indicating lower objective quality.
Effect is strongest in years when private firms are actively hiring, showing direct incentive for regulatory leniency.
biasFlags: Findings based on USPTO data; cross-sector generalizability debated.

entries.source: Moral Politics (George Lakoff) - Framing Research
entries.summary: Cognitive linguistic research on 'Strict Father' (GOP) vs 'Nurturant Parent' (Progressive) moral frameworks.
keyFacts: 'Strict Father' model views world as dangerous, promotes self-discipline through punishment (tough-on-crime laws).
'Nurturant Parent' model views world as community, promotes empathy and interdependence (social safety nets).
Republicans use 'Strict Father' language 3x more effectively than Democrats use their own metaphor in political advertisements.
biasFlags: Criticized by some linguists for over-simplifying ideological diversity into a binary model.

entries.source: The Race-Class Narrative (Shenker-Osorio & Haney Lopez)
entries.summary: Research into messaging that wins cross-racial support by explicitly naming race and class together to counter dog-whistle politics.
keyFacts: Messages that explicitly mention 'White, Black, and Brown' people working together outpoll 'colorblind' economic messages by 5-10 points.
61% of 'persuadable' voters in 2018 favored the Race-Class Narrative over traditional progressive or conservative 'zero-sum' stories.
Failing to mention race allows the right to 'fill the void' with racial scapegoating.
biasFlags: Commissioned by progressive organizations (SEIU, Demos) to inform political strategy.

entries.source: Red State Progressive Ballot Wins (2018-2024)
entries.summary: Data showing progressive policies winning in states that vote for Republican candidates.
keyFacts: 2018: Medicaid expansion passed in Idaho (61%), Nebraska (54%), Utah (53%) despite GOP executive opposition.
2024: Missouri (57%) and Alaska (56%) voters approved raising minimum wage to $15/hr.
2018: Florida voters passed Amendment 4 with 64.5% support to restore voting rights to 1.4M former felons.
biasFlags: Results reflect specific issue popularity, not necessarily a shift in partisan alignment.

entries.source: Deep Canvassing 2020 Results (People's Action)
entries.summary: First large-scale deep canvassing program during a presidential election - 102x more effective than TV persuasion.
keyFacts: Achieved a 3.1-point shift in vote margin for Biden among canvassed voters.
102 times more effective per person than average presidential TV persuasion program.
Most effective among Independent women (8.5-point impact) through 'story exchanges' rather than policy lectures.
biasFlags: Results measured in specific battleground states (MI, PA, WI); may not scale to all regions.

entries.source: Economic Populism Polling 2024 (Data for Progress)
entries.summary: 2024 polling showing progressive economic policies with supermajority support across party lines.
keyFacts: 83% of voters (including 74% of Republicans) support expanding Social Security benefits.
71% support raising federal minimum wage to at least $15/hr.
66% support a 'Wealth Tax' on individuals with more than $50M in assets.
biasFlags: Support levels drop significantly when tied to a specific partisan leader (the 'Sanders effect').

entries.source: Bernie Sanders Fox News Town Hall (2019)
entries.summary: Case study in cross-partisan messaging where a self-selected 'conservative' audience cheered for Medicare for All.
keyFacts: When Bret Baier asked for show of hands on 'willingly transition' to government-run system, large majority of audience cheered.
Sanders reframed loss of private insurance as gain in 'stability' - millions lose coverage every year due to job changes.
Post-event polling: Sanders' favorability among Fox viewers rose by 6 points immediately following the town hall.
biasFlags: Audience selected by Fox News but in Democratic-leaning area (Bethlehem, PA).

entries.source: The New Deal Infrastructure Legacy
entries.summary: Quantifiable physical accomplishments of the PWA, WPA, and CCC between 1933 and 1943.
keyFacts: WPA built 651,000 miles of roads, 78,000 bridges, and 125,000 civilian buildings.
CCC planted 3 billion trees and developed 800+ state parks across the US.
PWA funded the Hoover Dam, Lincoln Tunnel, and San Francisco Bay Bridge.
biasFlags: Historically contested on deficit spending grounds, though infrastructure value remains.

entries.source: Deficit Impact by President (1981-2024)
entries.summary: Specific deficit/surplus numbers by president showing Republican presidents consistently increase deficits despite 'small government' branding.
keyFacts: Bill Clinton ended term with $128B surplus (150% decrease in deficit); George W. Bush increased deficit by 1,204%.
Donald Trump increased national debt by $7.8 trillion (317% deficit increase) during four-year term.
Barack Obama decreased annual deficit by 53% from 2009 peak by end of term in 2017.
biasFlags: Deficits influenced by Congress and external shocks (recessions, COVID-19), not just the President.

entries.source: Union Era vs Post-Union Era Metrics (EPI)
entries.summary: The productivity-pay gap with specific numbers showing the divergence point.
keyFacts: 1948-1979 (High Union): productivity grew 108.1% and hourly compensation grew 93.2%.
1979-2022 (Low Union): productivity grew 64.7% while hourly compensation grew only 14.8%.
If minimum wage had kept pace with productivity since 1968, it would be over $24/hr today.
biasFlags: Decoupling attributed by some economists to technology/globalization rather than just union decline.

entries.source: Nordic Model vs US Health Outcomes (2023-2024 Data)
entries.summary: Specific per-capita cost and life expectancy comparisons between US and Nordic social democratic models.
keyFacts: 2023: US spent $13,432 per capita on health; Sweden spent $7,522, yet Sweden's life expectancy is 5 years longer (83.3 vs 78.4).
US infant mortality rate 5.4 per 1,000 births, nearly 3x higher than Norway (1.8) and Finland (1.9).
Nordic countries provide average 480 days paid parental leave (Sweden), compared to 0 federal days in US.
biasFlags: Comparisons critiqued due to US larger population and higher rates of lifestyle deaths (gun violence, opioids).

entries.source: Corporate Captured Science (Tobacco/Oil/Sugar Playbook)
entries.summary: The strategy of manufacturing doubt to delay regulation - same PR firm used by tobacco AND oil.
keyFacts: 1969: Internal tobacco memo: 'Doubt is our product since it is the best means of competing with the body of fact.'
Exxon hired the SAME PR firm (Hill & Knowlton) that tobacco industry used to dispute smoking-cancer link.
Sugar Research Foundation paid Harvard scientists in 1967 (~$50k today) to downplay sugar's link to heart disease and blame fat.
biasFlags: Historically verified through legal discovery in lawsuits.

entries.source: USDA Revolving Door (Tabakovic & Wollmann)
entries.summary: Research showing agribiotechnology firms use future employment offers to incentivize current regulators.
keyFacts: Firms saw crop approval times drop by 4.2 days in 2 years before they hired a USDA regulator.
Each day of delay costs a biotech firm up to $2M in revenue - 'revolving door' saves firms $8M+ per hire.
Analysis of 13,500 USDA planting approvals (1998-2017) confirmed this 'capture by future employment' pattern.
biasFlags: Focuses on single sector; cross-sector generalizability debated.

entries.source: Lakoff Framing in 60 Years of Campaign Ads
entries.summary: Quantitative analysis of Lakoff's 'Strict Father' vs 'Nurturant Parent' metaphors in presidential campaign ads 1952-2012.
keyFacts: Republicans used 'Strict Father' moral reasoning in 72% of their economic ads.
Democrats actively AVOIDED 'Nurturant Parent' reasoning in social program ads, often using Republican 'efficiency' frames instead.
Voters identify GOP as 'the party of the family' because they use familial metaphors 4x more often than Democrats.
biasFlags: Relies on human coding of 'moral metaphors' in vintage advertisements.

entries.source: Psychopathic Managers: Performance vs Promotion
entries.summary: Psychopathic managers get promoted despite poor objective performance because institutions mistake ruthlessness for leadership.
keyFacts: Psychopathic traits positively associated with high ratings in 'charisma' and 'strategic thinking' by superiors.
Same individuals received lowest scores in 'teamwork,' 'responsibility,' and 'actual technical performance'.
80% of psychopathic managers scored 'charismatic' but were 3x more likely involved in bullying and harassment complaints.
biasFlags: Sample of 203 managers is relatively small for broad-market conclusions.

entries.source: New Deal Job Programs: Scale and Speed
entries.summary: Scale of New Deal job programs for comparison to modern proposals like Green New Deal.
keyFacts: CWA (1933-34) employed 4 million people in first 3 months - hiring 3x faster than current private tech sector.
WPA cost $11 billion ($250B in 2024 dollars) over 8 years, supporting 8.5 million families during Great Depression.
PWA contributed $4 billion to tens of thousands of projects, 'heavy' infrastructure still in use today.
biasFlags: Economic impact debated by Austrian-school vs. Keynesian economists.

entries.source: GOP Now Defends Former 'Radical' Progressive Policies
entries.summary: How formerly 'radical' progressive policies became the 'third rail' of GOP politics with specific current data.
keyFacts: Social Security (1935): Now 90% support from GOP voters; Trump campaigned on 'not touching' it in 2016.
Medicare (1965): Republican presidential candidates in 2024 (e.g., Trump) frame themselves as 'protectors' of the program.
40-Hour Work Week (1938): Originally opposed by industry as 'communist,' now the standard protected by GOP base's 'work ethic' narrative.
biasFlags: While base voters defend these, GOP leadership periodically proposes privatization or eligibility age increases.

entries.source: CEO-to-Worker Pay Ratio Timeline 1965-2024 (EPI)
entries.summary: Specific CEO-to-worker pay ratios by year showing explosion from 21:1 in 1965 to 408:1 peak in 2021. CEO compensation grew 1,085% since 1978 while typical worker compensation grew 24-26%.
keyFacts: 1965: CEO-to-worker ratio was 21-to-1
1978: Ratio was 31-to-1
1989: Ratio was 61-to-1
1995: Ratio was 131-to-1
2000: Ratio hit 366-to-1 (dot-com peak)
2021: All-time high of 408-to-1
2024: Ratio at 281-to-1
1978-2024: CEO compensation grew 1,085% while typical worker compensation grew only 24-26%
biasFlags: Data from top 350 publicly traded firms - may not represent all companies. EPI is progressive-leaning but data methodology is transparent and widely cited.

entries.source: Corporate Psychopathy Prevalence - Multiple Studies
entries.summary: Three independent studies show psychopathic traits escalate dramatically with corporate rank: 1% general population, 4% management, 12% senior leadership, 21% executives. Psychopaths get promoted DESPITE poor performance because institutions mistake ruthlessness for decisiveness.
keyFacts: General population psychopathy rate: ~1% (Hare PCL-R)
Corporate management (Babiak/Hare 2010, n=203): 3.9-4% met psychopathy threshold
Corporate senior leadership (Croom, U of San Diego): ~12% display psychopathic traits
Corporate executives (Brooks, Bond/USD, n=261): 21% had 'clinically significant' psychopathic traits - same rate as prison populations
Brooks (forensic psychologist): Recruiters focus on factors other than personality, allowing 'successful psychopaths' into top roles
Paradox: Psychopathic professionals rated HIGH on charisma, creativity, strategic thinking - but LOW on responsibility, teamwork, and actual accomplishments
80% of psychopathic managers scored 'charismatic' but were 3x more likely involved in bullying and harassment
biasFlags: Studies have varying sample sizes and methodologies. The 21% figure (Brooks) uses a broader trait measure than the clinical PCL-R. Still, the gradient from 1% to 21% across multiple independent studies is striking.

entries.source: ExxonMobil Climate Denial - Full Dollar Amounts
entries.summary: Exxon's internal climate models from 1977-2003 were 63-83% accurate at predicting global warming. Meanwhile they spent $33M+ funding 60+ denial organizations. Internal strategy document defined 'victory' as making average citizens 'understand uncertainties in climate science.'
keyFacts: July 1977: Scientist James Black told Exxon management CO2 from fossil fuels was influencing global climate
1978: Black warned doubling CO2 would increase global temps by 2-3 degrees
1977-2002: 32 internal documents confirming climate change; 1982-2014: 72 peer-reviewed papers by Exxon scientists
Supran/Rahmstorf/Oreskes (Science, 2023): 63-83% of Exxon's internal projections accurately predicted subsequent warming
83% of Exxon's peer-reviewed internal papers acknowledged climate change was real; 81% of public advertorials expressed doubt
$33+ million to 60+ denial organizations since 1998 (ExxonSecrets database)
$8.67M to 40 organizations between 2000-2003 alone
$690,000 to 8 climate denier groups in 2019 - STILL funding denial after publicly acknowledging climate change
biasFlags: Exxon disputes cherry-picking of documents. However, the Science journal study and court proceedings make this among the most thoroughly documented corporate deception cases in history.

entries.source: Shenker-Osorio Race-Class Narrative - Detailed Methodology
entries.summary: Detailed findings from Race-Class Narrative project: 1,500+ adults surveyed. Explicitly mentioning race IMPROVED results even among persuadable Republicans. Colorblind economic populism ('just talk about jobs') was LESS effective than race-class framing. Core framework: Values, Villain, Vision.
keyFacts: Sample: 1,500 adults nationally + oversamples of 100 each: African Americans, Latinos, millennials, drop-off voters, unlikely voters
Voter breakdown: Base ~23%, Opposition ~18%, Persuadables ~59% (largest group by far)
Paradox: 63% of persuadables agreed 'Focusing on race doesn't fix anything' AND 77% of SAME group agreed 'Focusing on race is necessary to move forward'
ALL race-class narratives outperformed the Trumpian opposition message
All but one outperformed colorblind 'economic populism' - the approach long assumed best
Explicitly mentioning race improved results even among persuadable Republicans - contrary to conventional wisdom
Messages performed better when delivered by women and racially diverse leaders
Core framework: Values, Villain, Vision (in that order) - progressives typically fail by leading with problems
biasFlags: Research commissioned by progressive organizations (SEIU, Demos). Methodology is transparent but designed to inform progressive strategy specifically.

entries.source: Deficit by President - Specific Numbers (1981-2024)
entries.summary: All 4 Republican presidents since 1980 increased the deficit (Reagan +94%, Bush I +67%, Bush II +1,204%, Trump +317%). Both completed Democratic terms decreased it (Clinton -150% ending with $128B surplus, Obama -53% from peak). Pattern is unbroken across 44 years.
keyFacts: Reagan (R): Increased deficit +94%, average annual deficit -$176.5B (-4.1% GDP)
Bush I (R): Increased deficit +67%, average annual deficit -$258.9B (-4.1% GDP)
Clinton (D): Decreased deficit -150%, ended with $128B SURPLUS, average +$7.9B (+0.1% GDP)
Bush II (R): Increased deficit +1,204%, average annual deficit -$444.3B (-3.3% GDP)
Obama (D): Decreased annual deficit -53% from 2009 peak by end of term 2017
Trump I (R): Added $8.18T total debt (40% increase), deficit increase +317%
Pattern: Every Republican since 1980 increased deficit; every completed Democratic term decreased it
Pre-1980: All presidents Eisenhower through Nixon kept deficits under 2% of GDP
biasFlags: Deficits influenced by Congress, external shocks (recessions, wars, COVID), and inherited conditions - not just presidential policy. Pattern is real but causation is multi-factorial.

entries.source: Project 2025 - Stated Objectives and Structure
entries.summary: 920-page blueprint by 350+ conservative contributors with four pillars: policy guide, personnel database, training academy, and 180-day playbook. Core goal: 'deconstruct the administrative state' by replacing merit-based civil servants with political loyalists via Schedule F reclassification.
keyFacts: Full title: 'Mandate for Leadership: The Conservative Promise' - 920 pages, 30 chapters, one per federal department
Written by 350+ contributors coordinated by Heritage Foundation, published April 2023
Four pillars: (1) policy guide, (2) LinkedIn-style personnel database of vetted conservatives, (3) Presidential Administration Academy for training, (4) 180-day playbook
Schedule F: Reclassify tens of thousands of merit-based civil servants as political appointees - enables mass firing and replacement with loyalists
Agencies targeted for elimination: Department of Education (full dismantlement), DHS (restructuring)
Agencies targeted for partisan control: DOJ, FBI, FTC, FCC, Department of Commerce
Eliminate Head Start (serves 833,000+ children in poverty)
Project 2025 includes 900+ pages detailing replacement of 50,000 civil servants with ideologically aligned personnel
biasFlags: Objectives are stated in Project 2025's own language and documents. Framing as 'authoritarian' is contested by supporters who call it 'restoring executive power.' The structural parallels to historical authoritarian transitions are documented by multiple academic sources.

entries.source: Project 2025 Implementation Progress (Year 1)
entries.summary: 53% of Project 2025 domestic agenda initiated or completed in first 12 months (283 of 532 recommended actions). Key architects placed in power: Russell Vought (OMB), Peter Navarro (trade), Brendan Carr (FCC). Nearly two-thirds of early executive actions mirrored P2025 proposals.
keyFacts: 53% of domestic administrative policy agenda initiated or completed in first 12 months (Jan 2025-Jan 2026)
283 of 532 recommended actions put into action per Center for Progressive Reform tracker
47% implemented by October 2025, accelerating to 53% by January 2026
Nearly two-thirds of early executive actions mirrored or partially mirrored P2025 proposals
Russell Vought (P2025 architect) heads OMB - which he called 'the nerve center' of presidential influence
Peter Navarro (wrote P2025 trade section) is now top trade adviser
Brendan Carr (wrote FCC reform section) now chairs the FCC
DOGE created under Elon Musk - Heritage praised as 'welcome and long-overdue step'
biasFlags: Implementation tracking by Center for Progressive Reform (progressive organization). Percentage calculations depend on how 'recommended actions' are defined and counted. PBS/NPR coverage provides independent verification.

entries.source: Historical Parallels to Project 2025 - Comparative Analysis
entries.summary: Seven historical cases of systematic government restructuring compared to P2025. Closest parallels: Chile's El Ladrillo (pre-written blueprint for Day 1 implementation) and Hungary under Orban (Heritage Foundation's acknowledged model). P2025's 53% in 12 months is faster than Orban (~4 years) but within a system with more checks.
keyFacts: Nazi Germany Gleichschaltung (1933): Civil service purge (April 7, 1933) is structurally analogous to Schedule F. All formal opposition eliminated in 6 months. Unions abolished May 1933, all parties banned July 1933

Chile El Ladrillo (1973): Pre-written 11-chapter economic blueprint by Chicago Boys, ready before the coup. Implemented immediately under Pinochet. Closest structural parallel to P2025 - ideological outsiders write comprehensive plan, hand to incoming regime Day 1
Russia Putin (1999-2004): Independent media targeted first (NTV seized 2000), then oligarchs, then regional autonomy. ~5 years to consolidated autocracy

Hungary Orban (2010-2014): Heritage Foundation explicitly called Hungary 'THE model.' Danube Institute entered formal consulting agreement with Heritage. ~4 years to institutional capture. Now classified as 'electoral autocracy'
Turkey Erdogan (2002-2017): Gradual - initially democratic reformer, then reversed. 2016 failed coup used to purge ~150,000 civil servants, academics, judges. ~15 years total
Poland PiS (2015-2023): Judicial capture via forced retirement of ~two-thirds of Supreme Court judges. REVERSED by voters in 2023 - but institutional damage persists years later
Brazil Bolsonaro (2019-2022): Chaotic, no pre-written plan. REVERSED by voters in 2022. Institutions proved more resilient

Pattern: Countries with pre-written plans (Chile, P2025) implement faster. Countries with stronger institutions (Brazil, Poland) can reverse it. Media control is always either the first or second target
biasFlags: Comparing democratic governance changes to authoritarian transitions is contested. Supporters of P2025 argue these comparisons are alarmist. The structural parallels (civil service purges, institutional capture, media targeting) are documented but the degree of equivalence is debated.

entries.source: Trump Global Copycats - The Authoritarian Alliance
entries.summary: Global far-right leaders forming alliance around Trump-style authoritarian populism: Milei (Argentina), Meloni (Italy), Orban (Hungary), Wilders (Netherlands), AfD (Germany), Modi (India), Netanyahu (Israel), Farage (UK). Pattern: attack institutions, control media, replace civil servants. Some are backfiring - Canada rejected 'Maple MAGA' Poilievre after Trump's tariff aggression.
keyFacts: Milei (Argentina): Trump's 'favorite president.' Fired 37,000 public employees, 672 regulatory reforms. Poverty rate grew 11 percentage points in first six months - largest increase in 20 years
Vivek Ramaswamy explicitly cited Milei as model: 'A reasonable formula to fix the US government: Milei-style cuts, on steroids'
Musk told Milei: 'The example you are setting with Argentina will be a helpful model for the rest of the world'
Meloni (Italy): Labeled a 'dismantler' of rule of law. Press under 'unprecedented attacks.' Proposes constitutional 'premierato' concentrating executive authority
Musk posted 'Only the AfD can save Germany' - endorsement pattern same as with Milei/Trump
J.D. Vance at Munich Security Conference appeared to defend AfD, saying shutting people out of politics 'is the most surefire way to destroy democracy'
Reform UK established 'DOGE-style audit squads' to mirror Musk/Trump government-cutting model
Netanyahu: 2023 judicial reform to reduce Supreme Court oversight; seized AP News equipment; passed law to ban foreign media outlets (used against Al Jazeera)
biasFlags: 'Authoritarian alliance' framing used by progressive/academic sources. Supporters frame these leaders as democratic reformers challenging corrupt establishments. The factual actions (firing employees, restricting media, judicial restructuring) are documented regardless of framing.

entries.source: Poilievre 'Maple MAGA' - How Trump Killed Canadian Conservatism
entries.summary: Pierre Poilievre adopted Trump-style populist rhetoric, pledged to fire Bank of Canada governor, defund CBC, and use notwithstanding clause to override Charter rights. But Trump's tariff aggression against Canada backfired - anti-Trump sentiment surged, voters rejected Poilievre as 'Canada's Trump,' and he lost his own seat he'd held for two decades.
keyFacts: Poilievre adopted heightened crisis framing, combative media strategy, 'rage-based' messaging - majority of Canadians said his rhetoric reminded them of Trump
Pledged to fire governor of Bank of Canada and defund CBC - direct attacks on independent institutions
Pledged to use notwithstanding clause to override Charter rights - first Canadian PM candidate to do so federally
American right-wing media (Fox News) kept praising Poilievre even as he tried to distance from Trump
Trump's tariff aggression against Canada backfired on Poilievre - anti-Trump sentiment surged across Canada
Mark Carney (Liberal) won April 2025 election. Poilievre lost his own parliamentary seat he'd held for two decades
NBC headline: 'Canada was poised to elect its own Maple MAGA movement. Then Trump happened'
Academic analysis: Poilievre's core policies remained Harper-era neoliberalism, not Trumpian - populism was primarily in communication style, not substance
biasFlags: Canadian election outcome is factual. 'Maple MAGA' label is media/progressive framing. Academic analysis suggests Poilievre was less ideologically Trumpian than media portrayed - mainly adopted the communication style.

entries.source: Trump Tariff Capitulations - $1.5T+ Extracted From Allies
entries.summary: US average effective tariff rate rose from 2.5% to 27% (highest in over a century). Major allies capitulated with massive concessions: Japan $550B investment pledge, South Korea $350B, EU $600B. India agreed to stop buying Russian oil. Qatar donated a Boeing 747 as Air Force One. UK PM hand-delivered letter from King inviting Trump for state visit.
keyFacts: Jan-Apr 2025: US average effective tariff rate rose from 2.5% to 27% - highest in over a century
China: Tariff dropped from 125% to 10% after agreeing to purchase 12M metric tons US soybeans in 2025, 25 MMT/year 2026-2028
India: Tariff dropped from 50% to 18% after Modi pledged to stop buying Russian oil, reduce India's tariffs to zero, remove non-tariff barriers
Japan: Tariff dropped to 15% with $550B US investment commitment, market access for cars/trucks/energy/rice
South Korea: $350B investment pledge including energy procurement and shipbuilding contracts
EU: $600B investment into US energy and defense sectors for 15% tariff rate
Total investment pledges extracted from just three allies: $1.5 trillion+
Qatar donated a Boeing 747 to serve as Air Force One
biasFlags: Tariff rates and investment pledges are factual. Whether these represent 'capitulation' or 'pragmatic deal-making' depends on perspective. Some deals may benefit both parties. 'Liberation Day' framing is Trump administration language.

entries.source: Global Democratic Backsliding - 2024-2025 Index Data
entries.summary: Autocracies outnumber democracies for first time since 2002 (91 vs 88). 72% of world population lives under autocracies. US classified as 'electoral autocracy' by V-Dem since late 2025. US Century Foundation Democracy Meter: scored 57/100 in 2025, down from 79/100 in 2024 - a 22-point drop in one year. Press freedom at 50-year low globally.
keyFacts: V-Dem 2025: 91 autocracies vs 88 democracies - autocracies outnumber democracies for first time since 2002
72% of world population lives under autocracies (up from 71% in 2023)
45 countries actively autocratizing in 2024 (up from 12 countries twenty years ago). 25 started as democracies
Average level of liberal democracy back to 1985 levels (population-weighted)
Freedom of expression deteriorating in 44 countries - highest ever recorded
V-Dem director Staffan Lindberg: US undergoing 'fastest evolving episode of autocratization the USA has been through in modern history'
V-Dem classified US as 'electoral autocracy' since late 2025
Century Foundation Democracy Meter: US scored 57/100 in 2025, down from 79/100 in 2024 - 22-point drop in one year
biasFlags: V-Dem, Freedom House, and EIU are respected academic/research institutions but all have been accused of liberal bias by authoritarian-leaning governments. The US 'electoral autocracy' classification is particularly contested. Methodology differences between indices can produce different rankings. The trend data (direction of change) is more robust than absolute scores.

entries.source: 2025 Reversal - Trump Becoming Liability for Global Populists
entries.summary: By mid-2025, Trump's aggressive tariffs and confrontational stance toward allies started turning the populist wave into a 'dangerous undertow' for his imitators. Canada rejected Poilievre. Dutch coalition collapsed when Wilders quit. Global populists who emulated Trump began running from him as his policies hurt their own economies.
keyFacts: NPR (April 2025): Trump's tariffs and confrontational stance turning populist wave into 'dangerous undertow' for imitators
Canada: Poilievre rejected, lost his own seat after Trump tariff aggression against Canada
Netherlands: Wilders quit coalition June 3, 2025 over asylum plan rejection, forcing snap elections
Global populists who once emulated Trump began distancing as his policies hurt their economies and voters
Milei (Argentina): Accused of fraud for promoting cryptocurrency that collapsed, causing $251M losses for 86% of investors
Pattern emerging: Trump-style populism works as opposition rhetoric but creates governance crises when implemented
Brazil (2022) and Poland (2023) already demonstrated authoritarian populism can be reversed at the ballot box
biasFlags: 'Backlash' narrative may be premature - far-right parties remain strong in most countries despite individual setbacks. Selection bias in focusing on failures while ignoring continued success (Meloni still in power, AfD still rising).

entries.source: Every Accusation Is a Confession - The GOP Projection Pattern
entries.summary: Documented pattern where Republican accusations against opponents turn out to describe their own behavior. Weaponized projection works via cognitive overload, accusation fatigue, and well-poisoning. Goebbels principle: 'Always accuse your enemies of your own sins.' The pattern is so consistent it has predictive value.
keyFacts: Three Republican administrations (Nixon, Reagan, Trump 1st term): 142 criminal indictments. Three Democratic (Carter, Clinton, Obama): 2 criminal indictments
Broader analysis 1961-2016: Republican administrations had 18x more individuals indicted, 38x more convictions, 39x more prison sentences than Democratic administrations

Weaponized projection mechanism: (1) cognitive overload - confident accusers seem innocent, (2) boldness of lie creates twisted credibility, (3) poisons the well - real evidence becomes 'partisan tit-for-tat', (4) provides moral cover to keep doing it
Goebbels principle explicitly cited: 'Always accuse your enemies of your own sins'
Trump accused opponents of election fraud while pressuring Georgia to 'find 11,780 votes' and being indicted on 4 felony counts for overturning 2020 election
Trump deflected to Bill Clinton when Access Hollywood tape surfaced; 26+ women accused Trump of harassment/assault; jury found him liable for sexual abuse (E. Jean Carroll, 2023)
Trump called investigations 'witch hunts'; his allies Hunter and Collins both used same language, both pleaded guilty and were convicted
RationalWiki coined 'Haggard's Law': the louder one's objections to homosexuality, the more likely one is to be a closeted homosexual
biasFlags: The indictment comparison is factual but influenced by DOJ prosecution decisions and what constitutes 'administration' crimes. The projection pattern is well-documented but interpreting ALL accusations as confessions is an oversimplification - it's a strong heuristic, not a universal law.

entries.source: Anti-Gay Republicans Caught in Gay Scandals - 11 Documented Cases
entries.summary: At least 11 prominent anti-gay Republican politicians and allies caught in gay scandals while actively opposing LGBTQ rights. Pattern so consistent it has its own name (Haggard's Law). Includes a US Senator, a House Speaker's ally, state legislators, and a Trump campaign chair sentenced to 15 years for child sex trafficking.
keyFacts: Larry Craig (R-Idaho Senator): Voted consistently anti-gay; arrested 2007 soliciting male undercover cop in airport restroom, pleaded guilty to disorderly conduct
Ted Haggard (NAE President, Bush White House ally): Vocally opposed gay marriage; 2006 exposed paying male escort for sex and meth. Later admitted relationship with 20-year-old male church volunteer
Mark Foley (R-Florida Rep): Anti-gay legislative record; resigned 2006 after sending sexually explicit messages to teenage male congressional pages. Contributed to GOP losing the House
George Rekers (co-founded Family Research Council): Career opposing gay rights; caught 2010 returning from Europe with male escort from Rentboy.com. Claimed he hired him to 'carry his luggage'
Wes Goodman (R-Ohio State Rep): 'Family values' and 'faith-focused'; resigned 2017 after caught having sex with man in his legislative office

Ralph Shortey (R-Oklahoma State Senator, Trump campaign chair): Voted anti-LGBTQ; caught 2017 in motel with 17-year-old boy. Pleaded guilty to child sex trafficking. Sentenced to 15 years federal prison
Jim West (R-Mayor of Spokane): Supported banning gay workers at schools; 2005 caught seeking men on Gay.com. Also accused of molesting boys as Boy Scout leader. Recalled by voters
